questions.content: 2. Let 𝑥0 ∈ R and let 𝑓 be a function that is defined on a neighborhood of 𝑥0. Suppose that 𝑓 is differentiable at every 𝑥 in the neighborhood for which 𝑥 ≠ 𝑥0. Let 𝐿 ∈ R and suppose also that lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) = 𝐿. (a) Give an example of a function satisfying the conditions above, and such that 𝑓 is not differentiable at 𝑥0. Solution: Consider the function defined by 𝑓 (𝑥) =    0 𝑥 ≠ 0 1 𝑥 = 0 for every 𝑥 ∈ R. We choose 𝑥0 = 0. Note that for every 𝑥 ≠ 0, 𝑓 is differentiable by AOD at 𝑥 and that for every 𝑥 ≠ 0 we have 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) = 0. It follows that lim 𝑥→0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) = lim 𝑥→0 0 = 0 Note also that lim 𝑥→0 𝑓 (𝑥) = lim 𝑥→00 = 0 ≠ 1 = 𝑓 (0), which implies that 𝑓 is not continuous at 𝑥0 = 0 and therefore 𝑓 is not differentiable at 𝑥0 = 0. (b) Suppose, in addition, that 𝑓 is continuous at 𝑥0. Prove that 𝑓 is continuously differentiable at 𝑥0. Solution: As 𝑓 is continuous at 𝑥0, then lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 (𝑥) = 𝑓 (𝑥0). It follows that lim 𝑥→𝑥0 [ 𝑓 (𝑥) − 𝑓 (𝑥0)] AOL = 0 Thus, by L’Hˆopital’s rule 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0) = lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 (𝑥) − 𝑓 (𝑥0) 𝑥 − 𝑥0 0 0 = lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) 1 = lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) = 𝐿 It follows that 𝑓 is continuously differentiable at 𝑥0 and that 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0) = 𝐿 = lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥). (c) Conclude that there exists no function 𝑓 : (−1, 1) → R for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) =    𝑥 sin 1 𝑥   𝑥 ≠ 0 1 𝑥 = 0 for every 𝑥 ∈ (−1, 1). Solution: ATC that such a function exists. As 𝑓 is differentiable at 𝑥0 = 0 then 𝑓 is continuous at 𝑥0 = 0. As lim 𝑥→0 𝑓 (𝑥) = lim 𝑥→0[𝑥 sin 1 𝑥 ] 𝐵×𝑉 = 0, then by item (b), 𝑓 is continuously differentiable at 𝑥0 = 0 and therefore 1 = 𝑓 ′ (0) = lim 𝑥→0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) = 0 Contradiction. 3

questions.content: 3. Let 𝑥0 ∈ R and let 𝑓 : R → R. (a) Suppose that 𝑓 is twice-differentiable at 𝑥0. Prove that lim ℎ→0 𝑓 (𝑥0 + ℎ) − 2 𝑓 (𝑥0) + 𝑓 (𝑥0 − ℎ) ℎ2 = 𝑓 ′′ (𝑥0) Solution: As 𝑓 is twice differentiable, then 𝑓 is continuous and therefore lim ℎ→0 𝑓 (𝑥0 + ℎ) = 𝑓 (𝑥0) and lim ℎ→0 𝑓 (𝑥0 − ℎ) = 𝑓 (𝑥0). It follows that lim ℎ→0 [ 𝑓 (𝑥0 + ℎ) − 2 𝑓 (𝑥0) + 𝑓 (𝑥0 − ℎ)] AOL = 0. Also, since 𝑓 is twice differentiable, 𝑓 ′ is differentiable in a neighborhood of 𝑥0. We now compute the limit starting from the left-hand side: lim ℎ→0 𝑓 (𝑥0 + ℎ) − 2 𝑓 (𝑥0) + 𝑓 (𝑥0 − ℎ) ℎ2 0 0 = lim ℎ→0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0 + ℎ) + 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0 − ℎ) · (−1) 2ℎ = lim ℎ→0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0 + ℎ) − 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0 − ℎ) 2ℎ = lim ℎ→0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0 + ℎ) − 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0) + 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0) − 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0 − ℎ) 2ℎ = lim ℎ→0   𝑓 ′ (𝑥0 + ℎ) − 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0) 2ℎ + 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0) − 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0 − ℎ)) ℎ   (★) = 𝑓 ′′ (𝑥0) 2 + 𝑓 ′′ (𝑥0) 2 = 𝑓 ′′ (𝑥0) where lim ℎ→0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0 − ℎ) − 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0) ℎ 𝑡=−ℎ = lim 𝑡→0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0 + 𝑡) − 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0) −𝑡 = lim 𝑡→0 − 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0 + 𝑡) − 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0) 𝑡 AOL = − 𝑓 ′′ (𝑥0). (★) (b) Prove or disprove the following statement: If the limit lim ℎ→0 𝑓 ( 𝑥0+ℎ) −2 𝑓 ( 𝑥0 )+ 𝑓 ( 𝑥0 −ℎ) ℎ2 exists, then 𝑓 is twice-differentiable at 𝑥0. Solution: False. We choose 𝑓 (𝑥) =    𝑥2 2 𝑥 ≥ 0 − 𝑥2 2 𝑥 < 0 for every 𝑥 ∈ R and choose 𝑥0 = 0. We know show that 𝑓 is not twice differentiable at 𝑥0 = 0. Note that for every 𝑥 ∈ R we have 𝑓 (𝑥) + 𝑓 (−𝑥) = 0. It follows that lim ℎ→0 𝑓 (𝑥0 + ℎ) − 2 𝑓 (𝑥0) + 𝑓 (𝑥0 − ℎ) ℎ2 = lim ℎ→0 𝑓 (ℎ) + 𝑓 (−ℎ) ℎ2 = lim ℎ→0 0 ℎ2 = 0. 4

questions.content: 4. (a) Student A has computed lim 𝑥→1 𝑥3+3𝑥−4 𝑥2 −3𝑥+2 by using L’Hˆopital’s rule in the following way: lim 𝑥→1 𝑥3 + 3𝑥 − 4 𝑥2 − 3𝑥 + 2 = lim 𝑥→1 3𝑥2 + 3 2𝑥 − 3 = lim 𝑥→1 6𝑥 2 = 3 Explain student A’s mistake. Solution: Student A used L’Hˆopital’s rule but the conditions for L’Hˆopital’s rule don’t hold, as the limit lim 𝑥→1 3𝑥2+3 2𝑥−3 is not a limit of the form 0 0 nor of the form ? ∞ - it is a limit of the form 6 −1 . Student A could have used L’Hˆopital’s rule only on the first limit, but not on the second one. Indeed, the value of the limit is −6. (b) Student A has proved that every differentiable function 𝑓 : R → R must also be continuously differentiable in the following way: Let 𝑥0 ∈ R. Then 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0) def = lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 (𝑥) − 𝑓 (𝑥0) 𝑥 − 𝑥0 L’ˆopital 0 0 = lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) 1 = lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) Explain student A’s mistake. Solution: Student A assumed that lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) exists in the extended sense, which is one of the assumptions of L’Hˆopital’s rule, but this assumption doesn’t always hold. Indeed, the function defined by 𝑓 (𝑥) =    𝑥2 sin 1 𝑥   𝑥 ≠ 0 0 𝑥 = 0 for every 𝑥 ∈ R was seen in class as a function that is not continuously differentiable at 𝑥0 = 0, because lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) does not exist in the extended sense. (c) Student A found a counter-example for Darboux’s theorem: Consider the function defined by 𝑓 (𝑥) =    𝑥 𝑥 > 0 −𝑥 𝑥 < 0 Then 𝑓 is differentiable by AOD. Note that 𝑓 ′ (1) = 1 and 𝑓 ′ (−1) = −1, but there exists no 𝑐 ∈ [−1, 1] for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑐) = 0. Explain student A’s mistake. Solution: The conditions for Darboux’s theorem do not hold; the function is not differentiable on [−1, 1] as it is not defined at 𝑥0 = 0, and therefore not differentiable at 𝑥0 = 0. 5

questions.content: 5. Let 𝑓 be a function that is defined on (0, 2). Suppose that 𝑓 is differentiable. (a) Suppose that 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) · 𝑓 ′ 1 𝑥   < 0 for every 1 ≠ 𝑥 ∈ (0, 2). Prove that 𝑓 ′ (1) = 0. Solution: First note that as 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) · 𝑓 ′ 1 𝑥   < 0 for every 1 ≠ 𝑥 ∈ (0, 2), then 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) ≠ 0 for every 1 ≠ 𝑥 ∈ (0, 2). As 𝑓 ′ 2 3   · 𝑓 ′ 3 2   < 0, then by Darboux’s theorem there exists a 2 3 < 𝑐 < 3 2 for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑐) = 0. Thus, 𝑐 = 1. (b) Suppose that lim 𝑥→2− 𝑓 (𝑥) does not exist in the extended sense. Prove that there exists a 𝑐 ∈ (0, 2) for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑐) = 0. Solution: ATC that 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) ≠ 0 for every 0 < 𝑥 < 2. Suppose first that 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) > 0 for every 𝑥 ∈ (0, 2). Then 𝑓 is strictly increasing, and therefore lim 𝑥→2− 𝑓 (𝑥) exists in the extended sense and lim 𝑥→2− 𝑓 (𝑥) = sup { 𝑓 (𝑥) : 0 < 𝑥 < 2} Contradiction. Suppose now that 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) < 0 for every 𝑥 ∈ (0, 2). Then 𝑓 is strictly decreasing, and therefore lim 𝑥→2− 𝑓 (𝑥) exists in the extended sense and lim 𝑥→2− 𝑓 (𝑥) = inf { 𝑓 (𝑥) : 0 < 𝑥 < 2} Contradiction. We conclude that there exists two points 0 < 𝑥1 < 𝑥2 < 2 for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑥1) · 𝑓 ′ (𝑥2) < 0. As 𝑓 is differentiable on [𝑥1, 𝑥2], then by Darboux’s theorem there exists an 𝑥1 < 𝑥 < 𝑥2 for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) = 0. Contradiction. 6

Prove that there exists a 𝛿 > 0 such that for every 0 < 𝑥 < 𝛿 we have 𝑥 − 0.17𝑥3 < sin(𝑥) < 𝑥 − 0.16𝑥3 Solution: We compute lim 𝑥→0+ sin(𝑥) − 𝑥 𝑥3 0 0 = lim 𝑥→0+ cos(𝑥) − 1 3𝑥2 − 1 2 famous = − 1 6 As lim 𝑥→0+ sin( 𝑥) −𝑥 𝑥3 = − 1 6 , then there exists a 𝛿1 > 0 such that for every 0 < 𝑥 < 𝛿1 we have −0.17 < sin(𝑥) − 𝑥 𝑥3 < −0.16 (★) Choose 𝛿 = 𝛿1 and let 0 < 𝑥 < 𝛿. questions.content: (c) Let 𝑓 : R → R be a differentiable function. Consider the function defined by 𝑔(𝑥) = 𝑓 (𝑥) − 𝑓 (−𝑥) for every 𝑥 ∈ R. Suppose that lim 𝑥→∞𝑔(𝑥) = 0. Conclude, using items (a) and (b), that there exists a 𝑐 ∈ R for which 𝑔′ (𝑐) = 0. Solution: We first show that 𝑔 is an odd function: let 𝑥 ∈ R. We note that 𝑔(−𝑥) = 𝑓 (−𝑥) − 𝑓 (−(−𝑥)) = 𝑓 (−𝑥) − 𝑓 (𝑥) = −( 𝑓 (𝑥) − 𝑓 (−𝑥)) = −𝑔(𝑥) Since we assumed lim 𝑥→∞ 𝑔(𝑥) = 0 then by item (a) we also obtain lim 𝑥→−∞𝑔(𝑥) = −0 = 0. As 𝑓 is differentiable, then by AOD 𝑔 is differentiable and therefore continuous and therefore by item (b) there exists an 𝑐 ∈ R such that 𝑐 is an extreme point. As 𝑔 is differentiable, then by Fermat’s theorem we have 𝑔′ (𝑐) = 0. (d) Prove that there exists a 𝑑 ∈ R for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑑) = 0. Solution: By AOD, 𝑔 is differentiable 𝑔′ (𝑥) = 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) − 𝑓 ′ (−𝑥) · (−1) = 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) + 𝑓 ′ (−𝑥), ∀𝑥 ∈ R As 𝑔′ (𝑐) = 0, then 𝑓 ′ (𝑐) + 𝑓 ′ (−𝑐) = 0 ⇒ 𝑓 ′ (𝑐) = − 𝑓 ′ (−𝑐). If 𝑐 = −𝑐 then 𝑐 = 0 and therefore 𝑓 ′ (0) = − 𝑓 ′ (0) ⇒ 𝑓 ′ (0) = 0. We choose 𝑑 = 0 and complete the proof. Otherwise, 𝑐, −𝑐 are two different points. As 𝑓 ′ (−𝑐) = − 𝑓 ′ (𝑐), then 𝑓 ′ (𝑐) · 𝑓 ′ (−𝑐) = − [ 𝑓 ′ (𝑐)]2 ≤ 0 Thus, by the generalized Darboux theorem, there exists a 𝑑 ∈ R between −𝑐 and 𝑐 for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑑) = 0. 9

4. (a) Student A has computed lim 𝑥→1 𝑥3+3𝑥−4 𝑥2 −3𝑥+2 by using L’Hˆopital’s rule in the following way: lim 𝑥→1 𝑥3 + 3𝑥 − 4 𝑥2 − 3𝑥 + 2 = lim 𝑥→1 3𝑥2 + 3 2𝑥 − 3 = lim 𝑥→1 6𝑥 2 = 3 Explain student A’s mistake. Solution: Student A used L’Hˆopital’s rule but the conditions for L’Hˆopital’s rule don’t hold, as the limit lim 𝑥→1 3𝑥2+3 2𝑥−3 is not a limit of the form 0 0 nor of the form ? ∞ - it is a limit of the form 6 −1 . Student A could have used L’Hˆopital’s rule only on the first limit, but not on the second one. Indeed, the value of the limit is −6. (b) Student A has proved that every differentiable function 𝑓 : R → R must also be continuously differentiable in the following way: Let 𝑥0 ∈ R. Then 𝑓 ′ (𝑥0) def = lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 (𝑥) − 𝑓 (𝑥0) 𝑥 − 𝑥0 L’ˆopital 0 0 = lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) 1 = lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) Explain student A’s mistake. Solution: Student A assumed that lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) exists in the extended sense, which is one of the assumptions of L’Hˆopital’s rule, but this assumption doesn’t always hold. Indeed, the function defined by 𝑓 (𝑥) =    𝑥2 sin 1 𝑥   𝑥 ≠ 0 0 𝑥 = 0 for every 𝑥 ∈ R was seen in class as a function that is not continuously differentiable at 𝑥0 = 0, because lim 𝑥→𝑥0 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) does not exist in the extended sense. (c) Student A found a counter-example for Darboux’s theorem: Consider the function defined by 𝑓 (𝑥) =    𝑥 𝑥 > 0 −𝑥 𝑥 < 0 Then 𝑓 is differentiable by AOD. Note that 𝑓 ′ (1) = 1 and 𝑓 ′ (−1) = −1, but there exists no 𝑐 ∈ [−1, 1] for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑐) = 0. Explain student A’s mistake. Solution: The conditions for Darboux’s theorem do not hold; the function is not differentiable on [−1, 1] as it is not defined at 𝑥0 = 0, and therefore not differentiable at 𝑥0 = 0. 5

5. Let 𝑓 be a function that is defined on (0, 2). Suppose that 𝑓 is differentiable. (a) Suppose that 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) · 𝑓 ′ 1 𝑥   < 0 for every 1 ≠ 𝑥 ∈ (0, 2). Prove that 𝑓 ′ (1) = 0. Solution: First note that as 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) · 𝑓 ′ 1 𝑥   < 0 for every 1 ≠ 𝑥 ∈ (0, 2), then 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) ≠ 0 for every 1 ≠ 𝑥 ∈ (0, 2). As 𝑓 ′ 2 3   · 𝑓 ′ 3 2   < 0, then by Darboux’s theorem there exists a 2 3 < 𝑐 < 3 2 for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑐) = 0. Thus, 𝑐 = 1. (b) Suppose that lim 𝑥→2− 𝑓 (𝑥) does not exist in the extended sense. Prove that there exists a 𝑐 ∈ (0, 2) for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑐) = 0. Solution: ATC that 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) ≠ 0 for every 0 < 𝑥 < 2. Suppose first that 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) > 0 for every 𝑥 ∈ (0, 2). Then 𝑓 is strictly increasing, and therefore lim 𝑥→2− 𝑓 (𝑥) exists in the extended sense and lim 𝑥→2− 𝑓 (𝑥) = sup { 𝑓 (𝑥) : 0 < 𝑥 < 2} Contradiction. Suppose now that 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) < 0 for every 𝑥 ∈ (0, 2). Then 𝑓 is strictly decreasing, and therefore lim 𝑥→2− 𝑓 (𝑥) exists in the extended sense and lim 𝑥→2− 𝑓 (𝑥) = inf { 𝑓 (𝑥) : 0 < 𝑥 < 2} Contradiction. We conclude that there exists two points 0 < 𝑥1 < 𝑥2 < 2 for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑥1) · 𝑓 ′ (𝑥2) < 0. As 𝑓 is differentiable on [𝑥1, 𝑥2], then by Darboux’s theorem there exists an 𝑥1 < 𝑥 < 𝑥2 for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) = 0. Contradiction. 6

6. Let 𝐿 ∈ R. (a) Let 𝑔1 : R → R. Suppose 𝑔1 is an odd function, i.e., 𝑔1 (−𝑥) = −𝑔1 (𝑥) for every 𝑥 ∈ R. Suppose in addition that lim 𝑥→∞𝑔1 (𝑥) = 𝐿. Prove that lim 𝑥→−∞𝑔1 (𝑥) = −𝐿. Solution: We compute lim 𝑥→−∞𝑔1 (𝑥) 𝑡=−𝑥 = lim 𝑡→∞𝑔1 (−𝑡) odd = lim 𝑡→∞ − 𝑔1 (𝑡) AOL = −𝐿 (b) Let 𝑔2 : R → R be a continuous function. Prove that if lim 𝑥→∞𝑔2 (𝑥) = lim 𝑥→−∞𝑔2 (𝑥) = 𝐿, then 𝑔2 has an extreme point in R. Solution: First, if 𝑔2 is constant then every point is extreme. We now assume 𝑔2 is not constant and consider the function defined by Ψ(𝑥) =    𝑔2 (tan(𝑥)) − 𝜋 2 < 𝑥 < 𝜋 2 𝐿 𝑥 = 𝜋 2 𝐿 𝑥 = − 𝜋 2 , ∀𝑥 ∈ h − 𝜋 2 , 𝜋 2 i Note that as 𝑔2 is continuous and as lim 𝑥→∞𝑔2 (𝑥) = lim 𝑥→−∞𝑔2 (𝑥) = 𝐿, then lim 𝑥→ 𝜋 2 − Ψ(𝑥) = lim 𝑥→ 𝜋 2 − 𝑔2 (tan(𝑥)) 𝑦=tan( 𝑥) = lim 𝑦→∞ 𝑔2 (𝑦) = 𝐿 = Ψ   𝜋 2   and similarly, lim 𝑥→− 𝜋 2 + Ψ(𝑥) = lim 𝑥→− 𝜋 2 + 𝑔2 (tan(𝑥)) 𝑦=tan( 𝑥) = lim 𝑦→−∞ 𝑔2 (𝑦) = 𝐿 = Ψ   − 𝜋 2   It follows that Ψ is left-continuous at 𝜋 2 and right-continuous at − 𝜋 2 . As 𝑔2 is continuous, then Ψ is also continuous on − 𝜋 2 , 𝜋 2  . Thus Ψ is continuous on  − 𝜋 2 , 𝜋 2  . It follows that by Weirstrass’s theorem, Ψ attains a maximum and a minimum on  − 𝜋 2 , 𝜋 2  , namely, there exist 𝑥1, 𝑥2 ∈  − 𝜋 2 , 𝜋 2   such that Ψ(𝑥2) ≤ Ψ(𝑥) ≤ Ψ(𝑥1), ∀𝑥 ∈ h − 𝜋 2 , 𝜋 2 i As 𝑔2 is not constant, then there exist 𝑦1, 𝑦2 ∈ R such that 𝑦1 ≠ 𝑦2 and 𝑔(𝑦1) ≠ 𝑔(𝑦2). We note that there exist 𝑥1 = arctan(𝑦1), 𝑥2 = arctan(𝑦2). We note that 𝑥1, 𝑥2 ∈ − 𝜋 2 , 𝜋 2  . As 𝑦1 ≠ 𝑦2, then 𝑥1 ≠ 𝑥2. Thus Ψ(𝑥1) = 𝑔2 (tan(𝑥1)) = 𝑔2 (tan(arctan(𝑦1)) = 𝑔2 (𝑦1) ≠ 𝑔2 (𝑦2) = 𝑔2 (tan(arctan(𝑦2)) = 𝑔2 (tan(𝑥2)) = Ψ(𝑥2) Thus Ψ is not constant, and as Ψ − 𝜋 2   = Ψ 𝜋 2  , at least on of these extreme points lies in the interior of the interval, namely {𝑥1, 𝑥2 } ≠  − 𝜋 2 , 𝜋 2 . Denoting this point by 𝑥0, and defining 𝑦0 = tan(𝑥0) (well-defined as 𝑥0 ∈ − 𝜋 2 , 𝜋 2  ) then 𝑦0 is an extreme point of 𝑔2. 8

(c) Let 𝑓 : R → R be a differentiable function. Consider the function defined by 𝑔(𝑥) = 𝑓 (𝑥) − 𝑓 (−𝑥) for every 𝑥 ∈ R. Suppose that lim 𝑥→∞𝑔(𝑥) = 0. Conclude, using items (a) and (b), that there exists a 𝑐 ∈ R for which 𝑔′ (𝑐) = 0. Solution: We first show that 𝑔 is an odd function: let 𝑥 ∈ R. We note that 𝑔(−𝑥) = 𝑓 (−𝑥) − 𝑓 (−(−𝑥)) = 𝑓 (−𝑥) − 𝑓 (𝑥) = −( 𝑓 (𝑥) − 𝑓 (−𝑥)) = −𝑔(𝑥) Since we assumed lim 𝑥→∞ 𝑔(𝑥) = 0 then by item (a) we also obtain lim 𝑥→−∞𝑔(𝑥) = −0 = 0. As 𝑓 is differentiable, then by AOD 𝑔 is differentiable and therefore continuous and therefore by item (b) there exists an 𝑐 ∈ R such that 𝑐 is an extreme point. As 𝑔 is differentiable, then by Fermat’s theorem we have 𝑔′ (𝑐) = 0. (d) Prove that there exists a 𝑑 ∈ R for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑑) = 0. Solution: By AOD, 𝑔 is differentiable 𝑔′ (𝑥) = 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) − 𝑓 ′ (−𝑥) · (−1) = 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) + 𝑓 ′ (−𝑥), ∀𝑥 ∈ R As 𝑔′ (𝑐) = 0, then 𝑓 ′ (𝑐) + 𝑓 ′ (−𝑐) = 0 ⇒ 𝑓 ′ (𝑐) = − 𝑓 ′ (−𝑐). If 𝑐 = −𝑐 then 𝑐 = 0 and therefore 𝑓 ′ (0) = − 𝑓 ′ (0) ⇒ 𝑓 ′ (0) = 0. We choose 𝑑 = 0 and complete the proof. Otherwise, 𝑐, −𝑐 are two different points. As 𝑓 ′ (−𝑐) = − 𝑓 ′ (𝑐), then 𝑓 ′ (𝑐) · 𝑓 ′ (−𝑐) = − [ 𝑓 ′ (𝑐)]2 ≤ 0 Thus, by the generalized Darboux theorem, there exists a 𝑑 ∈ R between −𝑐 and 𝑐 for which 𝑓 ′ (𝑑) = 0. 9

questions.content: Calculus II – Spring 2025-26 Homework 2 Solution 1. Define each of the following terms. Give a full and complete mathematical definition. If your definition includes a secondary term that was defined in the course, you must define it as well! You do not have to define any term that appears prior to the words ’define the term’. You are not required to re-define terms that were already defined. Let (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 be a sequence of real numbers. (a) Define the term: the sequence (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 is not bounded from below and not bounded from above, without using any negation word. Solution: We say that the sequence (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 is not bounded from below and not bounded from above if for every 𝐾1 ∈ R there exists an 𝑛1 ∈ N for which 𝑎𝑛1 < 𝐾1 and for every 𝐾2 ∈ R there exists an 𝑛2 ∈ N for which 𝑎𝑛2 > 𝐾2. (b) Define the term: lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛 ≠ ∞, without using any negation word. Solution: We say that lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛 ≠ ∞ if there exists a 𝐾 > 0 such that for every 𝑁 ∈ N there exists an 𝑛 ≥ 𝑁 for which 𝑎𝑛 ≤ 𝐾. (c) Define the term: we have lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛 = −1. Solution: We say that lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛 = −1 if for every 𝜖 > 0 there exists an 𝑁 ∈ N such that for every 𝑛 ≥ 𝑁 we have |𝑎𝑛 + 1| < 𝜖. (d) Define the term: the sequence (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 is strictly monotone. Solution: We say that the sequence (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 is strictly monotone if for every 𝑛 ∈ N we have 𝑎𝑛 > 𝑎𝑛+1 or for every 𝑛 ∈ N we have 𝑎𝑛 < 𝑎𝑛+1. 1

questions.content: 2. Let (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 be a sequence of real numbers. Prove each of the following statements. In items (a) and (b) prove using only the definitions. (a) For every 𝑘 ∈ N, the sequence (𝑎𝑛+𝑘 )∞ 𝑛=1 is convergent if and only if (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 is convergent, and in that case, lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛+𝑘 = lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛. Solution: Let 𝑘 ∈ N. We define (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 = (𝑎𝑛+𝑘 )∞ 𝑛=1. ⇐: As (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 is convergent, then there exists an 𝐿 ∈ R such that lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛 = 𝐿. Let 𝜖 > 0. As lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛 = 𝐿, then there exists an 𝑁1 ∈ N such that for every 𝑛 ≥ 𝑁1 we have |𝑎𝑛 − 𝐿| < 𝜖 (★) We choose 𝑁 = 𝑁1 . Let 𝑛 ≥ 𝑁, therefore 𝑛 + 𝑘 ≥ 𝑁1 and therefore (★) holds for 𝑛 + 𝑘. Then |𝑏𝑛 − 𝐿| = |𝑎𝑛+𝑘 − 𝐿| < 𝜖 Thus (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=𝑘 is convergent and lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛 = lim 𝑛→∞𝑏𝑛 = 𝐿 . ⇒: As (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 is convergent, then there exists an 𝐿 ∈ R such that lim 𝑛→∞𝑏𝑛 = 𝐿. Let 𝜖 > 0. As lim 𝑛→∞𝑏𝑛 = 𝐿, then there exists an 𝑁1 ∈ N such that for every 𝑛 ≥ 𝑁1 we have |𝑏𝑛 − 𝐿| < 𝜖 (★★) We choose 𝑁 = 𝑁1 + 𝑘 . Let 𝑛 ≥ 𝑁, therefore 𝑛 − 𝑘 ≥ 𝑁1 and therefore (★★) holds for 𝑛 − 𝑘. Then |𝑎𝑛 − 𝐿| = |𝑏𝑛−𝑘 − 𝐿| < 𝜖 Thus (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 is convergent and lim 𝑛→∞𝑏𝑛 = lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛 = 𝐿. 2

Let 0 < 𝛽 < 𝛼 and let (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 and (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 be two sequences that are defined by the recursive formulas    𝑎𝑛+1 = 𝑎𝑛+𝑏𝑛 2 , 𝑏𝑛+1 = 2𝑎𝑛 𝑏𝑛 𝑎𝑛+𝑏𝑛 , ∀𝑛 ∈ N 𝑎1 = 𝛼, 𝑏1 = 𝛽 (a) Prove that (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 and (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 are well-defined, and prove that 𝑎𝑛+1 ≥ 𝑏𝑛+1 for every 𝑛 ∈ N. Solution: We first prove by induction that 𝑎𝑛, 𝑏𝑛 > 0 for every 𝑛 ∈ N. Base case: (𝑛 = 1). It is already given that 0 < 𝑏1 < 𝑎1. Induction step: (𝑛 ⇒ 𝑛 + 1.) Let 𝑛 ∈ N and suppose that 𝑎𝑛, 𝑏𝑛 > 0. Then 𝑎𝑛+1 = 𝑎𝑛+𝑏𝑛 2 > 0, 𝑏𝑛+1 = 2𝑎𝑛 𝑏𝑛 𝑎𝑛+𝑏𝑛 > 0. Thus (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 and (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 are well-defined. We prove now that 𝑎𝑛+1 ≥ 𝑏𝑛+1 for every 𝑛 ∈ N. Let 𝑛 ∈ N. As (𝑎𝑛 − 𝑏𝑛)2 ≥ 0 for every 𝑛 ∈ N, then 𝑎2 𝑛 − 2𝑎𝑛 𝑏𝑛 + 𝑏2 𝑛 ≥ 0 ⇒ 𝑎2 𝑛 + 2𝑎𝑛 𝑏𝑛 − 4𝑎𝑛 𝑏𝑛 + 𝑏2 𝑛 ≥ 0 ⇒ 𝑎2 𝑛 + 2𝑎𝑛 𝑏𝑛 + 𝑏2 𝑛 ≥ 4𝑎𝑛 𝑏𝑛 ⇒ (𝑎𝑛 + 𝑏𝑛) · (𝑎𝑛 + 𝑏𝑛) ≥ 4𝑎𝑛 𝑏𝑛 We divide both sides by 2 (𝑎𝑛 + 𝑏𝑛) and we obtain 𝑎𝑛+1 = 𝑎𝑛 + 𝑏𝑛 2 ≥ 2𝑎𝑛 𝑏𝑛 𝑎𝑛 + 𝑏𝑛 = 𝑏𝑛+1. (b) Prove that there exists an 𝐿 ∈ R such that lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛 = lim 𝑛→∞𝑏𝑛 = 𝐿. Solution: We prove that (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 is decreasing. Let 𝑛 ∈ N. We note that 𝑎𝑛+1 = 𝑎𝑛 + 𝑏𝑛 2 ≤ 𝑎𝑛 + 𝑎𝑛 2 = 𝑎𝑛 We conclude that 𝑎𝑛 ≤ 𝑎1 for every 𝑛 ∈ N. We prove that (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 is increasing. Let 𝑛 ∈ N. We note that 𝑏𝑛+1 = 2𝑎𝑛 𝑏𝑛 𝑎𝑛 + 𝑏𝑛 ≥ 2𝑎𝑛 𝑏𝑛 𝑎𝑛 + 𝑎𝑛 = 𝑏𝑛 We conclude that 𝑏𝑛 ≥ 𝑏1 for every 𝑛 ∈ N. Thus for every 𝑛 ∈ N we have 𝑏1 ≤ 𝑏𝑛 ≤ 𝑎𝑛 ≤ 𝑎1 Thus (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 is bounded from below by 𝑏1 and (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 is bounded from above by 𝑎1 and therefore there exist 𝐿1, 𝐿2 ∈ R such that lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛 = 𝐿1 and lim 𝑛→∞𝑏𝑛 = 𝐿2. 8

Solution: Consider the function defined by 𝑔(𝑥) = 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑥 , ∀𝑥 ∈ (0, ∞) Note that 𝑔 is differentiable by AOD and 𝑔′ (𝑥) = 𝑥 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) − 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑥2 , ∀𝑥 ∈ (0, ∞) By the assumption, the function (ln(𝑥) − 1) 𝑓 (𝑥) is an anti-derivative of the function ln(𝑥) 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) on the interval (0, ∞), namely [(ln(𝑥) − 1) 𝑓 (𝑥))]′ = ln(𝑥) 𝑓 ′ (𝑥), ∀𝑥 ∈ (0, ∞) On the other hand, by Leibniz rule [(ln(𝑥) − 1) 𝑓 (𝑥))]′ = 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑥 + (ln(𝑥) − 1) 𝑓 ′ (𝑥), ∀𝑥 ∈ (0, ∞) Equating, we obtain ln(𝑥) 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) = 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑥 + (ln(𝑥) − 1) 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) ⇒ 𝑥 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) − 𝑓 (𝑥) = 0, ∀𝑥 ∈ (0, ∞) Hence 𝑔′ (𝑥) = 𝑥 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) − 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑥2 = 0, ∀𝑥 ∈ (0, ∞) It follows that there exists an 𝛼 ∈ R such that for every 𝑥 ∈ (0, ∞), 𝑔(𝑥) = 𝛼 ⇒ 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑥 = 𝛼 ⇒ 𝑓 (𝑥) = 𝛼𝑥 5

questions.content: 5. (a) Student A has proved that 0 = 1 by using the method of integration by parts in the following way: ∫ 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 · ln(𝑥) 𝑢′= 1 𝑥 ,𝑣= 1 ln( 𝑥) = 1 ln(𝑥) · ln(𝑥) − ∫   ln(𝑥) ·   − 1 𝑥 · ln2 (𝑥)    𝑑𝑥 = 1 + ∫ 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 · ln(𝑥) subtracting ∫ 𝑑 𝑥 𝑥·ln( 𝑥) from both sides, we get 0 = 1. Explain Student A’s mistake. Solution: The value of an indefinite integral is not a particular value (i.e., not a number) but rather a range of values which differ by a constant. Thus, one should understand the equality above as equality up to a constant. It follows that the law of cancellation does not apply in his proof. (b) Student A proved that every bounded function is integrable on every interval [𝑎, 𝑏] in the following way: Let 𝑎, 𝑏 ∈ R such that 𝑎 < 𝑏. As 𝑓 is bounded on [𝑎, 𝑏], then there exists a 𝐾 > 0 such that | 𝑓 (𝑥)| ≤ 𝐾 for every 𝑥 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏]. For every 𝑛 we choose a partition 𝑃𝑛 = (𝑥0, . . . , 𝑥𝑛) of [𝑎, 𝑏] for which 𝑥𝑘+1 − 𝑥𝑘 < 1 𝐾 · 𝑛2 for every 0 ≤ 𝑘 ≤ 𝑛 − 1. Thus 0 ≤ 𝑆( 𝑓 , 𝑃𝑛) − 𝑆( 𝑓 , 𝑃𝑛) = 𝑛 ∑︁ 𝑘=1 𝑀𝑘 (𝑥𝑘+1 − 𝑥𝑘 ) − 𝑛 ∑︁ 𝑘=1 𝑚𝑘 (𝑥𝑘+1 − 𝑥𝑘 ) = 𝑛 ∑︁ 𝑘=1 (𝑀𝑘 − 𝑚𝑘 ) (𝑥𝑘+1 − 𝑥𝑘 ) < 𝑛 ∑︁ 𝑘=1 (𝑀𝑘 − 𝑚𝑘 ) 1 𝐾 · 𝑛2 ≤ 𝑛 ∑︁ 𝑘=1 2𝐾 1 𝐾 · 𝑛2 = 2 𝑛 ∑︁ 𝑘=1 1 𝑛2 = 2 𝑛 Thus, by the squeeze rule, we have lim 𝑛→∞ h 𝑆 ( 𝑓 , 𝑃𝑛) − 𝑆 ( 𝑓 , 𝑃𝑛) i = 0, from which follows that 𝑓 is integrable. Explain student A’s mistake. Solution: Student A is wrong, since a partition 𝑃𝑛 satisfying the requirements does not necessarily exist. Indeed, for the Dirichlet function 𝐷 and the interval [0, 1], there is no partition of size 𝑛 = 3 satisfying 𝑥𝑘+1 − 𝑥𝑘 < 1 9 for every 0 ≤ 𝑘 ≤ 2. 6

Let 𝑥0 ∈ R, let 𝜙 : R → [𝑎, 𝑏] and let 𝐺 : R → R be the function that is defined by 𝐺 (𝑥) = ∫ 𝜙 ( 𝑥) 𝑎 𝑓 (𝑠) 𝑑𝑠 for every 𝑥 ∈ R. (a) Prove that 𝐺 is well defined. Solution: As 𝜙 : R → [𝑎, 𝑏], then 𝜙(𝑥) ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏] for every 𝑥 ∈ R. As 𝑓 is integrable on [𝑎, 𝑏], then 𝑓 is integrable on [𝑎, 𝜙 (𝑥)] for every 𝑥 ∈ R and thus, 𝐺 is well defined in R. (b) Prove that if 𝑓 is continuous at 𝑦0 = 𝜙 (𝑥0) and if 𝜙 is differentiable at 𝑥0, then 𝐺 is differentiable at 𝑥0 and 𝐺′ (𝑥0) = 𝑓 (𝜙 (𝑥0)) · 𝜙′ (𝑥0). Solution: Consider the function that is defined by ℎ (𝑦) = ∫ 𝑦 𝑎 𝑓 (𝑠) 𝑑𝑠 for every 𝑦 ∈ [𝑎, 𝑏]. As 𝑓 is continuous at 𝜙 (𝑥0), then by the Fundamental theorem of calculus ℎ is differentiable at 𝜙 (𝑥0) and ℎ′ (𝜙 (𝑥0)) = 𝑓 (𝜙 (𝑥0)). N

questions.content: 2. Determine whether each of the following improper integrals converges or diverges (a) ∫ ∞ 1 𝑒−𝑥 ln(𝑥) 𝑑𝑥. Solution: First, we clearly have 𝑒−𝑥 ln(𝑥) ≥ 0 for every 𝑥 ≥ 1. Thus we can use the comparison test. Note that lim 𝑥→∞ 𝑥𝑒−𝑥 = lim 𝑥→∞ 𝑥 𝑒𝑥 ? ∞ = lim 𝑥→∞ 1 𝑒𝑥 = 0 (★) We know that ln(1 + 𝑥) ≤ 𝑥 for every 𝑥 > −1 .Thus, ln(𝑥) ≤ 𝑥 − 1 ≤ 𝑥 for every 𝑥 ≥ 1. We now compute π ∞ 1 𝑒−𝑥 ln(𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 ≤ π ∞ 1 𝑥𝑒−𝑥 𝑑𝑥 IBP = [−𝑥𝑒−𝑥 ]∞ 1 + π ∞ 1 𝑒−𝑥 𝑑𝑥 (★) = 1 𝑒 + [−𝑒−𝑥 ]∞ 1 = 2 𝑒 Thus, the integral converges by the comparison test. (b) ∫ ∞ 1 7𝑥+sin 𝑥 2𝑥3+𝑥+1 𝑑𝑥. Solution: First, we clearly have 7𝑥+sin 𝑥 2𝑥3+𝑥+1 ≥ 0 for every 𝑥 ≥ 1. Thus we can use the comparison test. We note that π ∞ 1 7𝑥 + sin 𝑥 2𝑥3 + 𝑥 + 1 𝑑𝑥 ≤ π ∞ 1 7𝑥 + 𝑥 2𝑥3 𝑑𝑥 = π ∞ 1 8𝑥 2𝑥3 𝑑𝑥 = π ∞ 1 4 𝑥2 𝑑𝑥 = 4 π ∞ 1 𝑑𝑥 𝑥2 < ∞ Thus, by the comparison test ∫ ∞ 1 7𝑥+sin 𝑥 2𝑥3+𝑥+1 𝑑𝑥 < ∞. (c) ∫ 3 1 𝑥 ln( 𝑥) 𝑑𝑥. Solution: First, we clearly have 𝑥 ln( 𝑥) ≥ 0 for every 1 < 𝑥 ≤ 3. Thus we can use the comparison test. We proved that for every 𝑥 > 1 we have ln (1 + 𝑥) ≤ 𝑥, thus ln (𝑥) ≤ 𝑥 − 1 ⇒ 1 ln 𝑥 ≥ 1 𝑥 − 1 and therefore π 3 1 𝑥 ln (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 ≥ π 3 1 𝑥 𝑥 − 1 𝑑𝑥 ≥ π 3 1 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 − 1 𝑡=𝑥−1,𝑑𝑡=𝑑 𝑥 = π 2 0 𝑑𝑡 𝑡 ≥ π 1 0 𝑑𝑡 𝑡 = ∞ Thus by the comparison test π 3 1 𝑥 ln (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 = ∞ 2

Prove there exists a point 𝑐 ∈ [0, 2] such that π 2 0 𝑥2 𝑓 (𝑥)𝑑𝑥 = 8 · 𝑓 (𝑐) 3 Solution: First, as 𝑓 is continuous on [0, 2], then 𝑓 is integrable on [0, 2] and therefore ∫ 2 0 𝑥2 𝑓 (𝑥)𝑑𝑥 is well-defined. As 𝑓 is continuous on [0, 2], then by Weierstrass’s theorem there exist 𝑥1, 𝑥2 ∈ [0, 2] such that 𝑓 (𝑥2) ≤ 𝑓 (𝑥) ≤ 𝑓 (𝑥1) ∀𝑥 ∈ [0, 2] We denote 𝑓 (𝑥1) = 𝑀 and 𝑓 (𝑥2) = 𝑚 and therefore 𝑚 ≤ 𝑓 (𝑥) ≤ 𝑀 ∀𝑥 ∈ [0, 2] and therefore 𝑚𝑥2 ≤ 𝑥2 𝑓 (𝑥) ≤ 𝑀𝑥2 ∀𝑥 ∈ [0, 2] Choose 𝑟 = 3 8 ∫ 2 0 𝑥2 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥. By the monotonicity of the definite integral, 𝑟 = 3 8 π 2 0 𝑥2 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 ≤ 3 8 π 2 0 𝑀𝑥2 𝑑𝑥 = 3𝑀 8 π 2 0 𝑥2 𝑑𝑥 = 3𝑀 8 · [ 𝑥3 3 ]2 0 = 3𝑀 8 · 8 3 = 𝑀 and similarly, 𝑟 ≥ 𝑚. It follows that 𝑚 ≤ 𝑟 ≤ 𝑀. If 𝑥1 = 𝑥2, then 𝑓 is constant and therefore for every 𝑐 ∈ [0, 2] we have 𝑓 (𝑐) = 𝑟 = 𝑚 = 𝑀 ⇒ ∫ 2 0 𝑥2 𝑓 (𝑥) = 8· 𝑓 (𝑐) 3 . Otherwise, as 𝑓 is continuous, then by the generalized IVT there exists a 𝑐 between 𝑥1 and 𝑥2 (namely, 𝑐 ∈ [𝑥1, 𝑥2] ⊆ [0, 2] or 𝑐 ∈ [𝑥2, 𝑥1] ⊆ [0, 2]) such that 𝑓 (𝑐) = 𝑟 ⇒ ∫ 2 0 𝑥2 𝑓 (𝑥) = 8· 𝑓 (𝑐) 3 . 4. Let 𝑓 : [1, ∞) → R be a differentiable positive function. Suppose that the improper integral ∫ ∞ 1 𝑓 ′ ( 𝑥) 𝑓 ( 𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 is convergent. Prove that lim 𝑥→∞ 𝑓 (𝑥) exists. Solution: We note that π ∞ 1 𝑓 ′ (𝑥) 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 = [ln ( 𝑓 (𝑥))]∞ 1 = lim 𝑥→∞ [ln( 𝑓 (𝑥)) − ln( 𝑓 (1))] As ∫ ∞ 1 𝑓 ′ ( 𝑥) 𝑓 ( 𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 is convergent, then lim 𝑥→∞ [ln( 𝑓 (𝑥)) − ln( 𝑓 (1))] exists. Thus lim 𝑥→∞ ln( 𝑓 (𝑥)) = lim 𝑥→∞ [ln( 𝑓 (𝑥)) − ln( 𝑓 (1)) + ln( 𝑓 (1))] exists by AOL, i.e., there exists an 𝐿 ∈ R such that lim 𝑥→∞ ln( 𝑓 (𝑥)) = 𝐿 and therefore lim 𝑥→∞ 𝑓 (𝑥) = lim 𝑥→∞ 𝑒ln( 𝑓 ( 𝑥) ) AOL = 𝑒

questions.content: 5. Let 𝑓 : [0, ∞) → R be a function that is integrable on [0, 𝑏] for every 𝑏 > 0. Prove or disprove each of the following statements: (a) Consider the function defined by 𝐹 (𝑥) = ∫ 𝑥 0 𝑓 (𝑡) 𝑑𝑡 for every 𝑥 ∈ [0, 1]. If 𝐹 is differentiable at 0, then 𝐹′ (0) = 𝑓 (0). Solution: False. We choose 𝑓 (𝑥) =    1 𝑥 = 0 0 𝑥 ≠ 0 ∀𝑥 ∈ [0, 1] Note that as 𝑓 differs from a continuous function by only 1 point, then 𝑓 is integrable on [0, 𝑥] for every 𝑥 ∈ [0, 1] and 𝐹 (𝑥) = π 𝑥 0 𝑓 (𝑡) 𝑑𝑡 = π 𝑥 0 0 𝑑𝑡 = 0 ∀𝑥 ∈ [0, 1] Thus, 𝐹 is differentiable at 0 and 𝐹′ (0) = 0 ≠ 𝑓 (0). (b) If ∫ 1 0 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 = 2.3, then there exists a 0 < 𝑐 < 1 for which ∫ 𝑐 0 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 = 𝑐 + 1. Solution: True. Consider the function defined by 𝐹 (𝑥) = ∫ 𝑥 0 𝑓 (𝑡) 𝑑𝑡 − 𝑥 − 1 for every 𝑥 ∈ [0, 1]. As 𝑓 is integrable on [0, 1] then 𝑓 is integrable on [0, 𝑥] for every 𝑥 ∈ [0, 1] and therefore 𝐹 is well defined. We also know that 𝐹 is continuous by AOC. Note that 𝐹 (0) = ∫ 0 0 𝑓 (𝑡) 𝑑𝑡 − 0 − 1 = −1 and that 𝐹 (1) = ∫ 1 0 𝑓 (𝑡) 𝑑𝑡 − 2 = 0.3. Thus, by the IVT, there exists a point 0 < 𝑐 < 1 for which 𝐹 (𝑐) = 0 ⇒ ∫ 𝑐 0 𝑓 (𝑡) 𝑑𝑡 = 𝑐 + 1. (c) Consider the function defined by 𝐹 (𝑥) = ∫ 1 𝑥 𝑓 (𝑡) 𝑑𝑡 for every 𝑥 ∈ [0, 1]. If 𝑓 is continuous at 0, then 𝐹′ (0) = − 𝑓 (0). Solution: True. Consider the function defined by 𝐺 (𝑥) = ∫ 𝑥 0 𝑓 (𝑡) 𝑑𝑡 for every 𝑥 ∈ [0, 1]. As 𝑓 is continuous at 𝑥0 = 0 then by the Fundamental theorem of Calculus, 𝐺 is differentiable at 𝑥0 = 0 and 𝐺′ (0) = 𝑓 (0). Note that for every 𝑥 ∈ [0, 1] we have 𝐹 (𝑥) = π 1 𝑥 𝑓 (𝑡) 𝑑𝑡 = π 1 0 𝑓 (𝑡) 𝑑𝑡 − π 𝑥 0 𝑓 (𝑡) 𝑑𝑡 = π 1 0 𝑓 (𝑡) 𝑑𝑡 − 𝐺 (𝑥) Thus, 𝐹 is differentiable at 𝑥0 = 0 by AOD and 𝐹′ (0) = −𝐺′ (0) = − 𝑓 (0). 4

Solution: For every 𝑛 ∈ N the function 𝑓 (𝑥) = sin𝑛 (𝑥) for every 𝑥 ∈ [0, 𝜋 2 ] is continuous by AOC, and therefore integrable on [0, 𝜋 2 ]. It follows that the integral 𝐼𝑛 = ∫ 𝜋 2 0 sin𝑛 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 exists, and consequently the sequence (𝐼𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 is well-defined. For 𝑛 = 0 it holds that 𝑓 (𝑥) = sin0 (𝑥) = 1 for every 0 < 𝑥 ≤ 𝜋 2 , and therefore 𝑓 has a removable discontinuity at 𝑥 = 0. Consequently, 𝑓 is integrable on [0, 𝜋 2 ] and therefore 𝐼0 is well-defined. (b) Prove that the sequence (𝐼𝑛)∞ 𝑛=0 satisfies the following recurrence formula    𝐼𝑛 = 𝑛−1 𝑛 𝐼𝑛−2 ∀𝑛 ≥ 2 𝐼0 = 𝜋 2 𝐼1 = 1 Solution: We first compute 𝐼0 = π 𝜋 2 0 sin0 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 = π 𝜋 2 0 1 𝑑𝑥 = 𝜋 2 and 𝐼1 = π 𝜋 2 0 sin(𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 = [− cos(𝑥)] 𝜋 2 0 = − cos ( 𝜋 2 ) + cos(0) = 1. We now prove that 𝐼𝑛 = 𝑛−1 𝑛 𝐼𝑛−2 for every 𝑛 ≥ 2. Let 𝑛 ≥ 2. We integrate by parts with 𝑢′ = sin 𝑥 and 𝑣 = sin𝑛−1 (𝑥). Then 𝐼𝑛 = π 𝜋 2 0 sin𝑛 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 = π 𝜋 2 0 sin(𝑥) · sin𝑛−1 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 IBP = [− cos(𝑥) · sin𝑛−1 (𝑥)] 𝜋 2 0 + (𝑛 − 1) π 𝜋 2 0 cos(𝑥) · sin𝑛−2 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 = (𝑛 − 1) π 𝜋 2 0 cos(𝑥) · sin𝑛−2 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 = (𝑛 − 1) π 𝜋 2 0 [1 − sin2 (𝑥)] · sin𝑛−2 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 = (𝑛 − 1) π 𝜋 2 0 sin𝑛−2 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 − (𝑛 − 1) π 𝜋 2 0 sin𝑛 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 = (𝑛 − 1)𝐼𝑛−2 − (𝑛 − 1)𝐼𝑛 Thus, 𝐼𝑛 = (𝑛 − 1)𝐼𝑛−2 − (𝑛 − 1)𝐼𝑛 ⇒ 𝑛𝐼𝑛 = (𝑛 − 1)𝐼𝑛−2 ⇒ 𝐼𝑛 = 𝑛−1 𝑛 𝐼𝑛−2. 6

questions.content: Calculus II – Spring 2025-26 Homework 6 Solution 1. Compute the value of each of the following series, or prove that it diverges. (a) ∞ ∑ 𝑛=0 (−1)𝑛 · 22𝑛+1 + (−1)𝑛 5𝑛 Solution: We have ∞ ∑ 𝑛=0 (−1)𝑛 · 22𝑛+1 + (−1)𝑛 5𝑛 = ∞ ∑ 𝑛=0 (−1)𝑛 · 2 · 4𝑛 5𝑛 + ∞ ∑ 𝑛=0 (−1)𝑛 · (−1)𝑛 5𝑛 = 2 · ∞ ∑ 𝑛=0 ( − 4 5 )𝑛 + ∞ ∑ 𝑛=0 ( 1 5 )𝑛 = 2 · 1 1 + 4 5 + 1 1 − 1 5 = 10 9 + 5 4 = 85 36 (b) ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 cosh ( 1 𝑛 ) , where cosh(𝑥) = 𝑒𝑥 + 𝑒−𝑥 2 , ∀𝑥 ∈ R Solution: Note that lim 𝑛→∞ 𝑎𝑛 = lim 𝑛→∞ cosh ( 1 𝑛 ) Heine’s lemma = lim 𝑥→0 cosh(𝑥) = lim 𝑥→0 𝑒𝑥 + 𝑒−𝑥 2 = 1 Thus, the series diverges by the necessary criterion. (c) ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑛2 − 1 𝑛3 Solution: Note that the series is non-negative. We have 𝐿 = lim 𝑛→∞ 𝑎𝑛 𝑏𝑛 = lim 𝑛→∞ [ 𝑛2 −1 𝑛3 ] [ 1 𝑛 ] = lim 𝑛→∞ 𝑛3 − 𝑛 𝑛3 = lim 𝑛→∞ [ 1 − 1 𝑛2 ] = 1 It follows that the series is divergent together with the Harmonic series. 1

We now write ∞ ∑ 𝑛=3 1 ln(𝑛) ≥ ∞ ∑ 𝑛=3 1 𝑛 tail of Harmonic = ∞ It follows that the series ∑∞ 𝑛=3 1 ln(𝑛) is divergent by the comparison test, and therefore the series ∑∞ 𝑛=2 1 ln(𝑛) is divergent as it has a divergent tail. (b) ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 (√𝑛 + 2 − √𝑛 )3 Solution: We note that ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 (√𝑛 + 2 − √𝑛 )3 = ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 ( 𝑛 + 2 − 𝑛 √𝑛 + 2 + √𝑛 )3 = ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 8 (√𝑛 + 2 + √𝑛 )3 We have = lim 𝑛→∞ [ 8 (√𝑛+2+√𝑛)3 ] [ 1 𝑛 3 2 ] = lim 𝑛→∞ 8𝑛 3 2 (√𝑛 + 2 + √𝑛 )3 = lim 𝑛→∞ 8 (√ 1 + 2 𝑛 + 1 )3 = 1 Thus, the series converges together with the 3 2 -Harmonic series. (c) ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 1 (𝑛 + 𝑒)𝑛 − 𝑒𝑛 Solution: By the comparison test ∞ ∑ 𝑛=3 1 (𝑛 + 𝑒)𝑛 − 𝑒𝑛 ≤ ∞ ∑ 𝑛=3 1 (𝑒 + 𝑒)𝑛 − 𝑒𝑛 = ∞ ∑ 𝑛=3 1 2𝑛 · 𝑒𝑛 − 𝑒𝑛 ≤ ∞ ∑ 𝑛=3 1 2 · 𝑒𝑛 − 𝑒𝑛 = ∞ ∑ 𝑛=3 1 𝑒𝑛 tail of geometric < ∞ It follows that ∑∞ 𝑛=3 1 (𝑛+𝑒)𝑛 −𝑒𝑛 < ∞ and therefore ∑∞ 𝑛=1 1 (𝑛+𝑒)𝑛 −𝑒𝑛 < ∞. 4

Solution: First, as 1 𝑛 ≤ 1 2 for every 2 ≤ 𝑛 ∈ N, then for every 2 ≤ 𝑛 ∈ N we have 𝜋 2 > 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 ≥ 2𝜋 4 − 2 4 𝜋>3>2 > 2𝜋 4 − 𝜋 4 = 𝜋 4 As tan is strictly increasing on [ 𝜋 4 , 𝜋 2 ), then for every 2 ≤ 𝑛 ∈ N tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 ) > tan ( 𝜋 4 ) = 1 As ln is increasing, then for every 2 ≤ 𝑛 ∈ N ln ( tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 )) > ln(1) = 0 and therefore the series is non-negative and well-defined. We note that lim 𝑥→∞ ln ( tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑥 )) 𝑡=tan( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑥 ) = lim 𝑡→∞ ln 𝑡 = ∞ We have lim 𝑛→∞ ( 1 ln(tan( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 )) ) ( 1 ln 𝑛 ) = lim 𝑛→∞ ln 𝑛 ln (tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 )) Heine’s lemma,𝑥𝑛=𝑛 = lim 𝑥→∞ ln 𝑥 ln (tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑥 )) ? ∞ = 𝐿 lim 𝑥→∞ ( 1 𝑥 ) ( 1 tan( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑥 ) ) · ( 1 cos2 ( 𝜋 2 )− 1 𝑥 ) · ( 1 𝑥2 ) = lim 𝑥→∞ 𝑥 tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑥 ) ( 1 cos2 ( 𝜋 2 )− 1 𝑥 ) = lim 𝑥→∞ [ 𝑥 tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑥 ) cos2 ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑥 )] = lim 𝑥→∞ [ 𝑥 · sin ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑥 ) cos ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑥 ) · cos2 ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑥 )] = lim 𝑥→∞ [ 𝑥 sin ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑥 ) cos ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑥 )] = lim 𝑥→∞ [ 𝑥 cos ( 1 𝑥 ) sin ( 1 𝑥 )] = lim 𝑥→∞ cos ( 1 𝑥 ) · sin ( 1 𝑥 ) ( 1 𝑥 ) 𝑡= 1 𝑥 = lim 𝑡→0+ [ cos 𝑡 · sin 𝑡 𝑡 ] = 1 · 1 = 1. It follows that the series diverges together with the series ∑∞ 𝑛=2 1 ln(𝑛) . 5

questions.content: Alternative solution (after showing that the series is non-negative and well-defined): As 𝑥 cos 𝑥 ≤ sin 𝑥 for every 0 < 𝑥 < 𝜋 2 , then cos 𝑥 sin 𝑥 ≤ 1 𝑥 for every 0 < 𝑥 < 𝜋 2 . As, for every 2 ≤ 𝑛 ∈ N we have 0 < 1 𝑛 ≤ 1 2 𝜋>3>1 < 𝜋 2 , then for every 2 ≤ 𝑛 ∈ N we have tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 ) = cot ( 1 𝑛 ) = cos ( 1 𝑛 ) sin ( 1 𝑛 ) ≤ 1 ( 1 𝑛 ) = 𝑛 As ln is strictly increasing, then for every 2 ≤ 𝑛 ∈ N ln ( tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 )) ≤ ln 𝑛 and therefore (both sides are positive) 1 ln (tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 )) ≥ 1 ln 𝑛 thus ∞ ∑ 𝑛=2 1 ln (tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 )) ≥ ∞ ∑ 𝑛=2 1 ln 𝑛 = ∞ Thus the series ∑∞ 𝑛=2 1 ln(tan( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 )) diverges. (e) ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 ∫ 1 𝑛 0 sin2 (𝑡) 𝑡 𝑑𝑡 First prove that the sequence is well-defined. Solution: First, as lim 𝑡→0 sin2 (𝑡 ) 𝑡 0 0 = lim 𝑡→0 2 sin(𝑡 ) cos(𝑡 ) 1 = 0, then the function defined by 𝑓 (𝑡) = sin2 (𝑡 ) 𝑡 for every 𝑡 > 0 has a removable discontinuity at 𝑡 = 0. Thus, the integral ∫ 1 𝑛 0 sin2 (𝑡 ) 𝑡 𝑑𝑡 is well-defined for every 𝑛 ∈ N. As sin(𝑡) ≤ 𝑡 for every 𝑡 > 0, then sin2 (𝑡 ) 𝑡 ≤ 𝑡 for every 𝑡 > 0. By the monotonicity of the definite integral (the point 𝑡 = 0 doesn’t affect the integral), we have 0 ≤ ∫ 1 𝑛 0 sin2 (𝑡) 𝑡 𝑑𝑡 ≤ ∫ 1 𝑛 0 𝑡 𝑑𝑡 = [ 𝑡2 2 ] 1 𝑛 0 = 1 2𝑛2 , ∀𝑛 ∈ N Thus, by the comparison principle we have ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 ∫ 1 𝑛 0 sin2 (𝑡) 𝑡 𝑑𝑡 ≤ ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 1 2𝑛2 = 1 2 ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 1 𝑛2 < ∞ 6

As, for every 2 ≤ 𝑛 ∈ N we have 0 < 1 𝑛 ≤ 1 2 𝜋>3>1 < 𝜋 2 , then for every 2 ≤ 𝑛 ∈ N we have tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 ) = cot ( 1 𝑛 ) = cos ( 1 𝑛 ) sin ( 1 𝑛 ) ≤ 1 ( 1 𝑛 ) = 𝑛 As ln is strictly increasing, then for every 2 ≤ 𝑛 ∈ N ln ( tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 )) ≤ ln 𝑛 and therefore (both sides are positive) 1 ln (tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 )) ≥ 1 ln 𝑛 thus ∞ ∑ 𝑛=2 1 ln (tan ( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 )) ≥ ∞ ∑ 𝑛=2 1 ln 𝑛 = ∞ Thus the series ∑∞ 𝑛=2 1 ln(tan( 𝜋 2 − 1 𝑛 )) diverges. (e) ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 ∫ 1 𝑛 0 sin2 (𝑡) 𝑡 𝑑𝑡 First prove that the sequence is well-defined. Solution: First, as lim 𝑡→0 sin2 (𝑡 ) 𝑡 0 0 = lim 𝑡→0 2 sin(𝑡 ) cos(𝑡 ) 1 = 0, then the function defined by 𝑓 (𝑡) = sin2 (𝑡 ) 𝑡 for every 𝑡 > 0 ha

questions.content: 3. (a) Student A has proved that ∫ ∞ 1 𝑑 𝑥 𝑥 = 0 in the following way: ∫ ∞ 1 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 = ∫ 2 1 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 + ∫ 3 2 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 + ∫ 4 3 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 + · · · = HHH ln(2) − ln(1) + HHH ln(3) − HHH ln(2) + HHH ln(4) − HHH ln(3) + ... = 0 Explain student A’s mistake. Solution: Student A made the following errors in his proof: i. Student A did not explain why ∫ ∞ 1 𝑑 𝑥 𝑥 = ∫ 2 1 𝑑 𝑥 𝑥 + ∫ 3 2 𝑑 𝑥 𝑥 + ∫ 4 3 𝑑 𝑥 𝑥 + . . . This equality assumes that ∫ ∞ 1 𝑑 𝑥 𝑥 converges in the extended sense, which renders the proof circular. ii. The equality ∫ ∞ 1 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 = ∫ 2 1 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 + ∫ 3 2 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 + ∫ 4 3 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 + · · · = ln(2) − ln(1) + ln(3) − ln(2) + ln(4) − ln(3) + ... is a mistake, as he should have added parenthesis on every two terms, namely, ∫ 2 1 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 + ∫ 3 2 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 + ∫ 4 3 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 + ... = (ln 2 − ln 1) + (ln 3 − ln 2) + (ln 4 − ln 3) + ... iii. Student A ignored the definition of an infinite sum as a limit of finite sums. What he should have is (ln 2 − ln 1) + (ln 3 − ln 2) + (ln 4 − ln 3) + ... = ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 (ln (𝑛 + 1) − ln 𝑛) = lim 𝑁 →∞ 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 (ln (𝑛 + 1) − ln 𝑛) = lim 𝑁 →∞ (ln (𝑁 + 1) − ln 1) = ∞ (b) Student A has proved that ∑∞ 𝑛=0 1 2𝑛 = 2 in the following way: Denote 𝑠 = ∑∞ 𝑛=0 1 2𝑛 = 1 + 1 2 + 1 4 + . . . . Then 𝑠 − 1 = 1 2 + 1 4 + . . . . Thus, 2(𝑠 − 1) = 1 + 1 2 + · · · = 𝑠. It follows that 2𝑠 − 2 = 𝑠. Thus 𝑠 = 2. Inspired by his method, student A also proved that ∑∞ 𝑛=0 2𝑛 = −1 in the following way: Denote 𝑠 = ∑∞ 𝑛=0 2𝑛 = 1 + 2 + 4 + . . . . Then 𝑠 − 1 = 2 + 4 + 8 + . . . . Thus, 1 2 (𝑠 − 1) = 1 + 2 + 4 + · · · = 𝑠. It follows that 1 2 (𝑠 − 1) = 𝑠. Thus 𝑠 = −1. Find the mistake of student A. Solution: Student A assumed that the series is convergent, but while this assertion is true for the first series (geometric with 𝑞 = 1 2 ) i

questions.content: 4. Let (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 be a sequence of real numbers. Consider the sequence defined by 𝑏𝑛 =    𝑎𝑘 𝑛 = 2𝑘 − 1 −𝑎𝑘 𝑛 = 2𝑘 ∀𝑛 ∈ N Prove that the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 is convergent if and only if lim 𝑛→∞𝑏𝑛 = 0. Solution: ⇒: As ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 is convergent then lim 𝑛→∞𝑏𝑛 = 0 by the necessary criterion. ⇐: As lim 𝑛→∞𝑏𝑛 = 0, then by the inheritance theorem we have lim 𝑘→∞𝑎𝑘 = 0. Consider the sequence defined by 𝑆𝑁 = 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 = 𝑏1 + 𝑏2 + . . . 𝑏𝑁 , ∀𝑁 ∈ N Note that 𝑆2𝑁 = 𝑎1 − 𝑎1 + 𝑎2 − 𝑎2 + · · · + 𝑎𝑁 − 𝑎𝑁 = 0, ∀𝑁 ∈ N and therefore lim 𝑁 →∞𝑆2𝑁 = 0. Note also that 𝑆2𝑁 = 𝑆2𝑁 −1 + 𝑏2𝑁 = 𝑆2𝑁 −1 − 𝑎𝑁 ⇒ 𝑆2𝑁 −1 = 𝑆2𝑁 + 𝑎𝑁 , ∀𝑁 ∈ N and therefore lim 𝑁 →∞𝑆2𝑁 −1 = lim 𝑁 →∞ [𝑆2𝑁 + 𝑎𝑁 ] = 0 + 0 = 0. It follows that lim 𝑁 →∞𝑆𝑁 = 0 and therefore ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 = lim 𝑁 →∞ 𝑆𝑁 = 0 8

sourceSnippet.text: 4. Let (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 be a sequence of real numbers. Consider the sequence defined by 𝑏𝑛 =    𝑎𝑘 𝑛 = 2𝑘 − 1 −𝑎𝑘 𝑛 = 2𝑘 ∀𝑛 ∈ N Prove that the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 is convergent if and only if lim 𝑛→∞𝑏𝑛 = 0. Solution: ⇒: As ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 is convergent then lim 𝑛→∞𝑏𝑛 = 0 by the necessary criterion. ⇐: As lim 𝑛→∞𝑏𝑛 = 0, then by the inheritance theorem we have lim 𝑘→∞𝑎𝑘 = 0. Consider the sequence defined by 𝑆𝑁 = 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 = 𝑏1 + 𝑏2 + . . . 𝑏𝑁 , ∀𝑁 ∈ N Note that 𝑆2𝑁 = 𝑎1 − 𝑎1 + 𝑎2 − 𝑎2 + · · · + 𝑎𝑁 − 𝑎𝑁 = 0, ∀𝑁 ∈ N and therefore lim 𝑁 →∞𝑆2𝑁 = 0. Note also that 𝑆2𝑁 = 𝑆2𝑁 −1 + 𝑏2𝑁 = 𝑆2𝑁 −1 − 𝑎𝑁 ⇒ 𝑆2𝑁 −1 = 𝑆2𝑁 + 𝑎𝑁 , ∀𝑁 ∈ N and therefore lim 𝑁 →∞𝑆2𝑁 −1 = lim 𝑁 →∞ [𝑆2𝑁 + 𝑎𝑁 ] = 0 + 0 = 0. It follows that lim 𝑁 →∞𝑆𝑁 = 0 and therefore ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 = lim 𝑁 →∞ 𝑆𝑁 = 0 8

questions.content: 5. Let (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 be a sequence of real numbers such that lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛 = 0. Let 𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐 ∈ R such that 𝑎 + 𝑏 + 𝑐 = 0. Let (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 be a sequence of real numbers such that for every 𝑛 ∈ N we have 𝑏𝑛 = 𝑎 · 𝑎𝑛 + 𝑏 · 𝑎𝑛+1 + 𝑐 · 𝑎𝑛+2. Prove that the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 converges and find its value. Solution: As 𝑎 + 𝑏 + 𝑐 = 0, then 𝑐 = −𝑎 − 𝑏, thus for every 𝑛 ∈ N we have 𝑏𝑛 = 𝑎 · 𝑎𝑛 + 𝑏 · 𝑎𝑛+1 + (−𝑎 − 𝑏) · 𝑎𝑛+2 = 𝑎 · 𝑎𝑛 + 𝑏 · 𝑎𝑛+1 − 𝑎 · 𝑎𝑛+2 − 𝑏𝑎𝑛+2 = 𝑎 (𝑎𝑛 − 𝑎𝑛+2) + 𝑏 (𝑎𝑛+1 − 𝑎𝑛+2) We note that for every 𝑛 ∈ N we have 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 = 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 [𝑎 (𝑎𝑛 − 𝑎𝑛+2) + 𝑏 (𝑎𝑛+1 − 𝑎𝑛+2)] = 𝑎 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 (𝑎𝑛 − 𝑎𝑛+2)+𝑏 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 (𝑎𝑛+1 − 𝑎𝑛+2) = 𝑎 (𝑎1 + 𝑎2 − 𝑎𝑁 +1 − 𝑎𝑁 +2)+𝑏 (𝑎2 − 𝑎𝑁 +2) As lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛 = 0, then lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛+1 = lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛+2 = 0 and therefore ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 = lim 𝑁 →∞ 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 = lim 𝑁 →∞ [𝑎 (𝑎1 + 𝑎2 − 𝑎𝑁 +1 − 𝑎𝑁 +2) + 𝑏(𝑎2 − 𝑎𝑁 +2)] AOL = 𝑎 · (𝑎1 + 𝑎2) + 𝑏 · 𝑎2 Thus the series converges and its value is 𝑎 · (𝑎1 + 𝑎2) + 𝑏 · 𝑎2. 9

sourceSnippet.text: 5. Let (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 be a sequence of real numbers such that lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛 = 0. Let 𝑎, 𝑏, 𝑐 ∈ R such that 𝑎 + 𝑏 + 𝑐 = 0. Let (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 be a sequence of real numbers such that for every 𝑛 ∈ N we have 𝑏𝑛 = 𝑎 · 𝑎𝑛 + 𝑏 · 𝑎𝑛+1 + 𝑐 · 𝑎𝑛+2. Prove that the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 converges and find its value. Solution: As 𝑎 + 𝑏 + 𝑐 = 0, then 𝑐 = −𝑎 − 𝑏, thus for every 𝑛 ∈ N we have 𝑏𝑛 = 𝑎 · 𝑎𝑛 + 𝑏 · 𝑎𝑛+1 + (−𝑎 − 𝑏) · 𝑎𝑛+2 = 𝑎 · 𝑎𝑛 + 𝑏 · 𝑎𝑛+1 − 𝑎 · 𝑎𝑛+2 − 𝑏𝑎𝑛+2 = 𝑎 (𝑎𝑛 − 𝑎𝑛+2) + 𝑏 (𝑎𝑛+1 − 𝑎𝑛+2) We note that for every 𝑛 ∈ N we have 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 = 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 [𝑎 (𝑎𝑛 − 𝑎𝑛+2) + 𝑏 (𝑎𝑛+1 − 𝑎𝑛+2)] = 𝑎 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 (𝑎𝑛 − 𝑎𝑛+2)+𝑏 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 (𝑎𝑛+1 − 𝑎𝑛+2) = 𝑎 (𝑎1 + 𝑎2 − 𝑎𝑁 +1 − 𝑎𝑁 +2)+𝑏 (𝑎2 − 𝑎𝑁 +2) As lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛 = 0, then lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛+1 = lim 𝑛→∞𝑎𝑛+2 = 0 and therefore ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 = lim 𝑁 →∞ 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 = lim 𝑁

questions.content: 6. Let (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 be two sequences of real numbers. Prove or disprove each of the following statements: (a) Suppose that (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 are non-negative. Suppose that 𝑎𝑛 < 𝑏𝑛 for every 𝑛 ∈ N and ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 < ∞. Then ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 < ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛. Solution: True. As 𝑎𝑛 < 𝑏𝑛 for every 𝑛 ∈ N, then 𝑎1 < 𝑏1. As ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 < ∞, then ∑∞ 𝑛=2 𝑏𝑛 < ∞. As 𝑎𝑛 < 𝑏𝑛 for every 𝑛 ∈ N and ∑∞ 𝑛=2 𝑏𝑛 < ∞, then by the comparison principle ∑∞ 𝑛=2 𝑎𝑛 < ∞ and ∞ ∑ 𝑛=2 𝑎𝑛 ≤ ∞ ∑ 𝑛=2 𝑏𝑛 < ∞ thus 𝑎1 + ∞ ∑ 𝑛=2 𝑎𝑛 < 𝑏1 + ∞ ∑ 𝑛=2 𝑏𝑛 ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 < ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 (b) Consider the sequence defined by 𝑆𝑁 = ∑𝑁 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 for every 𝑁 ∈ N. Suppose that 𝑏𝑛 = 𝑆2 𝑛 − 2𝑆𝑛−1 + 3 for every 2 ≤ 𝑛 ∈ N. If the series ∑∞ 𝑛=2 𝑏𝑛 is convergent, then the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 is divergent. Solution: True. ATC that the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 is convergent. Then ∃𝑠 ∈ R such that lim 𝑛→∞ 𝑆𝑛 = 𝑠. As the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 is convergent, then by the necessary criterion lim 𝑛→∞ 𝑏𝑛 = 0. Thus, by AOL, 0 = lim 𝑛→∞ 𝑏𝑛 = lim 𝑛→∞ ( 𝑆2 𝑛 − 2𝑆𝑛−1 + 3 ) = 𝑠2 − 2𝑠 + 3 Contradiction, as the equation 𝑠2 − 2𝑠 + 3 = 0 has no real solutions. 10

sourceSnippet.text: 6. Let (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 be two sequences of real numbers. Prove or disprove each of the following statements: (a) Suppose that (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 are non-negative. Suppose that 𝑎𝑛 < 𝑏𝑛 for every 𝑛 ∈ N and ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 < ∞. Then ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 < ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛. Solution: True. As 𝑎𝑛 < 𝑏𝑛 for every 𝑛 ∈ N, then 𝑎1 < 𝑏1. As ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 < ∞, then ∑∞ 𝑛=2 𝑏𝑛 < ∞. As 𝑎𝑛 < 𝑏𝑛 for every 𝑛 ∈ N and ∑∞ 𝑛=2 𝑏𝑛 < ∞, then by the comparison principle ∑∞ 𝑛=2 𝑎𝑛 < ∞ and ∞ ∑ 𝑛=2 𝑎𝑛 ≤ ∞ ∑ 𝑛=2 𝑏𝑛 < ∞ thus 𝑎1 + ∞ ∑ 𝑛=2 𝑎𝑛 < 𝑏1 + ∞ ∑ 𝑛=2 𝑏𝑛 ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 < ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑏𝑛 (b) Consider the sequence defined by 𝑆𝑁 = ∑𝑁 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 for every 𝑁 ∈ N. Suppose that 𝑏𝑛 = 𝑆2 𝑛 − 2𝑆𝑛−1 + 3 for every 2 ≤ 𝑛 ∈ N. If the series ∑∞ 𝑛=2 𝑏𝑛 is convergent, then the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 is divergent. Solution: True. ATC that the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 is convergent. Then ∃

questions.content: (c) Suppose that ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 is convergent. Then the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎2𝑛 is convergent. Solution: False. Consider the sequence defined by 𝑎𝑛 =    1 𝑘 𝑛 = 2𝑘 − 1 − 1 𝑘 𝑛 = 2𝑘 ∀𝑛 ∈ N Note that lim 𝑘→∞ 𝑎2𝑘−1 = lim 𝑘→∞ 1 𝑘 = 0, and lim 𝑘→∞ 𝑎2𝑘 = lim 𝑘→∞ ( − 1 𝑘 ) = 0 It follows that lim 𝑛→∞ 𝑎𝑛 = 0, and therefore, by Q4, the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 is convergent. However ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑎2𝑛 = ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 ( − 1 𝑛 ) minus Harmonic = diverges (d) Suppose that ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 is convergent. Then the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎2𝑛−1+𝑎2𝑛 2 is convergent. Solution: True. First, as the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 is convergent then there exists an 𝑠 ∈ R such that 𝑠 = lim 𝑁 →∞ 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛. Consider the sequence (𝑆𝑁 )∞ 𝑁 =1 defined by 𝑆𝑁 = 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛, ∀𝑁 ∈ N. Then lim 𝑁 →∞ 𝑆𝑁 = 𝑠, and therefore, by the Inheritance theorem, lim 𝑁 →∞ 𝑆2𝑁 = 𝑠. It follows that ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 𝑎2𝑛−1 + 𝑎2𝑛 2 = lim 𝑁 →∞ [ 𝑎1 + 𝑎2 2 + 𝑎3 + 𝑎4 2 + · · · + 𝑎2𝑁 −1 + 𝑎2𝑁 2 ] = lim 𝑁 →∞ 1 2 [(𝑎1 + 𝑎2) + (𝑎3 + 𝑎4) + · · · + (𝑎2𝑁 −1 + 𝑎2𝑁 ] = lim 𝑁 →∞ 1 2 [(𝑎1 + 𝑎2 + · · · + 𝑎2𝑁 ] = lim 𝑁 →∞ 1 2 𝑆2𝑁 AOL = 1 2 𝑠. (b) ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 2 · 4 · 6 · · · 2𝑛 𝑛𝑛 Solution: We use the ratio test 𝐿 = lim 𝑛→∞ 𝑎𝑛+1 𝑎𝑛 = lim 𝑛→∞ [ 2·4·6···2𝑛· (2𝑛+2) (𝑛+1)𝑛+1 ] [ 2·4·6···2𝑛 𝑛𝑛 ] = lim 𝑛→∞ (2𝑛 + 2) · 𝑛𝑛 (𝑛 + 1)𝑛+1 = lim 𝑛→∞ 2 · ( 𝑛 𝑛 + 1 )𝑛 = lim 𝑛→∞ 2 · ( 𝑛 + 1 𝑛 ) −𝑛 = lim 𝑛→∞ 2 · ( 1 + 1 𝑛 ) −𝑛 = 2 𝑒 < 1 It follows that the series is convergent. 1

(d) ∞ ∑ 𝑛=1 [ 𝑛2𝑛 · ( 1 − cos ( 1 𝑛 ))𝑛 ] Solution: We use the root test 𝐿 = lim 𝑛→∞ 𝑛 √𝑎𝑛 = lim 𝑛→∞ [ 𝑛2 · ( 1 − cos ( 1 𝑛 ))] = lim 𝑛→∞ 1 − cos ( 1 𝑛 ) ( 1 𝑛2 ) Heine = lim 𝑥→0 1 − cos(𝑥) 𝑥2 = lim 𝑥→0 [ − cos(𝑥) − 1 𝑥2 ] − 1 2 famous = 1 2 < 1 Thus, the series is convergent. (e) ∞ ∑ 𝑛=2 𝑛 √𝑛2 · 𝑎𝑛 𝑛1.5 , where 𝑎 ≥ 0 Solution: We use the root test 𝐿 = lim 𝑛→∞ 𝑛 √𝑎𝑛 = lim 𝑛→∞ 𝑛 √ 𝑛 √𝑛2 · 𝑎𝑛 𝑛1.5 = lim 𝑛→∞ 𝑛2√𝑛2 · 𝑎 ( 𝑛 √𝑛)1.5 = 𝑎 It follows that the series converges for 0 ≤ 𝑎 < 1 and diverges for 𝑎 > 1. For 𝑎 = 1 the series is ∑∞ 𝑛=2 𝑛√𝑛2 𝑛1.5 , and it converges with the 1.5-Harmonic series as 𝐿 = lim 𝑛→∞ 𝑎𝑛 𝑏𝑛 = lim 𝑛→∞ [ 𝑛√𝑛2 𝑛1.5 ] [ 1 𝑛1.5 ] = lim 𝑛→∞ ( 𝑛 √𝑛)2 = 1 Thus, the series is convergent if and only if 0 ≤ 𝑎 ≤ 1. 2

Explain student A’s mistake. Solution: Student A abused the algebra of limits. He used the algebra of limits on a number of functions that is not fixed, but depends on 𝑛. However, when using AOL, one needs to determine the number of functions and only then use the theorem, independently of 𝑛. 2. Find all values of 𝑝 ≥ 0 for which the following series is convergent ∞ ∑ 𝑛=3 1 𝑛 · ln(𝑛) · ln(ln(𝑛)) · [ln(ln(ln(𝑛)))] 𝑝 Solution: Let 𝑝 ≥ 0 and consider the function defined by 𝑓 (𝑥) = 1 𝑥 · ln(𝑥) · ln(ln(𝑥)) · [ln(ln(ln(𝑥)))] 𝑝 ∀𝑥 ≥ 27 Note that 𝑥 ≥ 27 = 33 ⇒ 𝑥 > 𝑒𝑒 ⇒ ln(𝑥) > 𝑒 ⇒ ln(ln(𝑥)) > 1 ⇒ ln(ln(ln(𝑥)) > 0 Thus, 𝑓 is well defined. We observe that since 𝑝 ≥ 0, we have lim 𝑥→∞ 𝑓 (𝑥) = 1 ∞ = 0. Also, note that 𝑓 is decreasing, since 𝑥 · ln(𝑥) · ln(ln(𝑥)) · [ln(ln(ln(𝑥)))] 𝑝 is increasing and positive for every 𝑥 ≥ 27. Thus, by the integral test, the series is convergent if and only if ∫ ∞ 27 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 is convergent. We compute ∫ ∞ 27 𝑓 (𝑥) 𝑑𝑥 = ∫ ∞ 27 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 · ln(𝑥) · ln(ln(𝑥)) · [ln(ln(ln(𝑥)))] 𝑝 𝑡=ln( 𝑥)⇒𝑑 𝑥=𝑥𝑑𝑡 = ∫ ∞ ln(27) 𝑑𝑡 𝑡 · ln(𝑡) · [𝑙𝑛(ln(𝑡))] 𝑝 𝑢=ln(𝑡 )⇒𝑑𝑡=𝑡 𝑑𝑢 = ∫ ∞ ln(ln(27) ) 𝑑𝑢 𝑢 · [ln(𝑢)] 𝑝 𝑠=ln(𝑢)⇒𝑑𝑢=𝑠𝑑𝑠 = ∫ ∞ ln(ln(ln(27) ) ) 𝑑𝑠 𝑠𝑝 Thus, the series is convergent if and only if 𝑝 > 1. 3

Let (𝑇𝑁 )∞ 𝑁 =1 be the sequence of real numbers defined by 𝑇𝑁 = ∑𝑁 𝑛=1 1 𝑛 − ∫ 𝑁 1 𝑑 𝑥 𝑥 for every 𝑁 ∈ N. (a) Prove that (𝑇𝑁 )∞ 𝑁 =1 is decreasing. Then 𝑇𝑁 +1 − 𝑇𝑁 = 𝑁 +1 ∑ 𝑛=1 1 𝑛 − ∫ 𝑁 +1 1 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 − ( 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 1 𝑛 − ∫ 𝑁 1 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 ) = 1 𝑁 + 1 − ∫ 𝑁 +1 1 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 + ∫ 𝑁 1 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 = 1 𝑁 + 1 − ∫ 𝑁 +1 𝑁 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 ≤ (★★) 1 𝑁 + 1 − 1 𝑁 + 1 = 0 Thus (𝑇𝑁 )∞ 𝑁 =1 is decreasing. (b) Conclude that there exists a constant 𝛾 ≥ 0 for which 𝛾 = lim 𝑁 →∞ [∑𝑁 𝑛=1 1 𝑛 − ln 𝑁]. Solution: Let 𝑁 ∈ N. Then 𝑇𝑁 = 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 1 𝑛 − ∫ 𝑁 1 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 = 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 ( 1 𝑛 − ∫ 𝑛+1 𝑛 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 ) + ∫ 𝑁 +1 𝑁 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 ≥ (★) 0 + ∫ 𝑁 +1 𝑁 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 ≥ 0 thus (𝑇𝑁 )∞ 𝑁 =1 is bounded from below by 0 and therefore lim 𝑁 →∞𝑇𝑁 exists, and by the monotonicity of limits we get that lim 𝑁 →∞𝑇𝑁 ≥ lim 𝑁 →∞0 = 0 We note that for every 𝑁 ∈ N we have ∫ 𝑁 1 𝑑𝑥 𝑥 = [ln | 𝑥 |] 𝑁 1 = ln 𝑁 − ln 1 = ln 𝑁 thus there exists an 𝛾 ≥ 0 for which 𝛾 = lim 𝑁 →∞ [ 𝑁 ∑ 𝑛=1 1 𝑛 − ln(𝑁) ] 4

questions.content: 4. Prove or disprove each of the following statements: (a) Let (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 be a sequence of non-negative numbers. If lim 𝑛→∞ (𝑛 · 𝑎𝑛) = 0, then ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 < ∞. Solution: False. We choose 𝑎1 = 1 and 𝑎𝑛 = 1 𝑛·ln 𝑛 for every 2 ≤ 𝑛 ∈ N. We note that lim 𝑛→∞ (𝑛 · 𝑎𝑛) = lim 𝑛→∞ [ 𝑛 · 1 𝑛 · ln 𝑛 ] = lim 𝑛→∞ 1 ln 𝑛 = 0 but we proved in class that ∞ ∑ 𝑛=2 1 𝑛 · ln 𝑛 = ∞ Thus ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 = ∞, since it has a divergent tail. (b) Let (𝑎𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 be a sequence of positive numbers. Suppose that 𝑎𝑛+1 𝑎𝑛 ≤ 𝑛2 (𝑛 + 1)2 ∀𝑛 ∈ N then the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 is convergent. Solution: True. Consider the sequence (𝑏𝑛)∞ 𝑛=1 defined by 𝑏𝑛 = 1 𝑛2 for every 𝑛 ∈ N. Note that for every 𝑛 ∈ N we have 𝑎𝑛+1 𝑎𝑛 ≤ 𝑛2 (𝑛 + 1)2 = [ 1 (𝑛+1)2 ] [ 1 𝑛2 ] = 𝑏𝑛+1 𝑏𝑛 Since the 2-Harmonic series is convergent, the series ∑∞ 𝑛=1 𝑎𝑛 is convergent. 5

Explain student A’s mistake. Solution: Student A abused the algebra of limits. He used the algebra of limits on a number of functions that is not fixed, but depends on 𝑛. However, when using AOL, one needs to determine the number of functions and only then use the theorem, independently of 𝑛. 2.
